answer_key.q1: A. Non-identical twins come from different eggs.
answer_key.q2: B. Genetic relation between identical twins is closer than non-identical ones.
answer_key.q3: D. Genetic influence to smoking is superior to environment’s.

passages.title: Twin Study: Two of a Kind
passages.source_html:
Twin Study: Two of a Kind


A


THE scientific study of twins goes back to the late 19th century, when Francis Galton, an early geneticist, realized that they came in two varieties: identical twins born from one egg and non-identical twins that had come from two. That insight turned out to be key, although it was not until 1924 that it was used to formulate what is known as the twin rule of pathology, and twin studies really got going.


B


The twin rule of pathology states that any heritable disease will be more concordant (that is, more likely to be jointly present or absent) in identical twins than in non-identical twins – and, in turn, will be more concordant in non-identical twins than in non-siblings. Early work, for example, showed that the statistical correlation of skin-mole counts between identical twins was 0.4, while non-identical twins had a correlation of only 0.2. (A score of 1.0 implies a perfect correlation, while a score of zero implies no correlation.) This result suggests that moles are heritable, but it also implies that there is an environmental component to the development of moles, otherwise the correlation in identical twins would be close to 1.0.


C


Twin research has shown that whether or not someone takes up smoking is determined mainly by environmental factors, but once he does so, how much he smokes is largely down to his genes. And while a person's religion is clearly a cultural attribute, there is a strong genetic component to religious fundamentalism. Twin studies are also unraveling the heritability of various aspects of human personality. Traits from neuroticism and anxiety to thrill- and novelty-seeking all have large genetic components. Parenting matters, but it does not determine personality in the way that some had thought.


D


More importantly, perhaps, twin studies are helping the understanding of diseases such as cancer, asthma, osteoporosis, arthritis and immune disorders. And twins can be used, within ethical limits, for medical experiments. A study that administered vitamin C to one twin and a placebo to the other found that it had no effect on the common cold. The lesson from all today's twin studies is that most human traits are at least partially influenced by genes. However, for the most part, the age-old dichotomy between nature and nurture is not very useful. Many genetic programs are open to input from the environment, and genes are frequently switched on or off by environmental signals. It is also possible that genes themselves influence their environment. Some humans have an innate preference for participation in sports. Others are drawn to novelty. Might people also be drawn to certain kinds of friends and types of experience? In this way, a person's genes might shape the environment they act in as much as the environment shapes the actions of the genes.


E


In the past, such research has been controversial. Josef Mengele, a Nazi doctor working at the Auschwitz extermination camp during the Second World War, was fascinated by twins. He sought them out among arrivals at the camp and preserved them from the gas-chambers for a series of brutal experiments. After the war, Cyril Burt, a British psychologist who worked on the heredity of intelligence, tainted twin research with results that appear, in retrospect, to have been rather too good. Some of his data on identical twins who had been reared apart were probably faked. In any case, the prevailing ideology in the social sciences after the war was Marxist, and disliked suggestions that differences in human potential might have underlying genetic causes. Twin studies were thus viewed with suspicion.


F


The ideological pendulum has swung back; however, as the human genome project and its aftermath have turned genes from abstract concepts to real pieces of DNA. The role of genes in sensitive areas such as intelligence is acknowledged by all but a few die-hards. The interesting questions now concern how nature and nurture interact to produce particular bits of biology, rather than which of the two is more important. Twin studies, which are a good way to ask these questions, are back in fashion, and many twins are enthusiastic participants in this research.


G


Research at the Twinsburg festival began in a small way, with a single stand in 1979. Gradually, news spread, and more scientists began turning up. This year, half a dozen groups of researchers were lodged in a specially pitched research tent. In one corner of this tent, Paul Breslin, who works at the Monell Institute in Philadelphia, watched over several tables where twins sat sipping clear liquids from cups and making notes. It was the team's third year at Twinsburg. Dr Breslin and his colleagues want to find out how genes influence human perception, particularly the senses of smell and taste and those (warmth, cold, pain, tingle, itch and so on) that result from stimulation of the skin. Perception is an example of something that is probably influenced by both genes and experience. Even before birth, people are exposed to flavours such as chocolate, garlic, mint and vanilla that pass intact into the bloodstream, and thus to the fetus. Though it is not yet clear whether such pre-natal exposure shapes taste-perception, there is evidence that it shapes preferences for foods encountered later in life.


H


However, there are clearly genetic influences at work, as well-for example in the ability to taste quinine. Some people experience this as intensely bitter, even when it is present at very low levels. Others, whose genetic endowment is different, are less bothered by it. Twin studies make this extremely clear. Within a pair of identical twins, either both, or neither, will find quinine hard to swallow. Non-identical twins will agree less frequently.


I


On the other side of the tent Dennis Drayna, from the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders, in Maryland, was studying hearing. He wants to know what happens to sounds after they reach the ear. It is not clear, he says, whether sound is processed into sensation mostly in the ear or in the brain. Dr Drayna has already been involved in a twin study which revealed that the perception of musical pitch is highly heritable. At Twinsburg, he is playing different words, or parts of words, into the left and right ears of his twinned volunteers. The composite of the two sounds that an individual reports hearing depends on how he processes this diverse information and that, Dr Drayna believes, may well be influenced by genetics.


J


Elsewhere in the marquee, Peter Miraldi, of Kent State University in Ohio, was trying to find out whether genes affect an individual's motivation to communicate with others. A number of twin studies have shown that personality and sociability are heritable, so he thinks this is fertile ground. And next to Mr. Miraldi was a team of dermatologists from Case Western Reserve University in Cleveland. They are looking at the development of skin disease and male-pattern baldness. The goal of the latter piece of research is to find the genes responsible for making men's hair fall out.


K


The busiest part of the tent, however, was the queue for forensic-science research into fingerprints. The origins of this study are shrouded in mystery. For many months, the festival's organisers have been convinced that the Secret Service – the American government agency responsible for, among other things, the safety of the president – is behind it. When The Economist contacted the Secret Service for more information, we were referred to Steve Nash, who is chairman of the International Association for Identification (IAI), and is also a detective in the scientific investigations section of the Marin County Sheriff's Office in California. The IAI, based in Minnesota, is an organisation of forensic scientists from around the world. Among other things, it publishes the Journal of Forensic Identification.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14-18 


The reading passage has eleven paragraphs A-K. Which paragraph contains the following information? Write the correct letter A-K, in boxes 14-18 on your answer sheet. 


NB You may use any letter more than once


Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
14

14 Mentioned research conducted in Ohio
✅ 正确答案：J

原文答案句：Elsewhere in the marquee, Peter Miraldi, of Kent State University in Ohio, was trying to find out whether genes affect an individual’s motivation to communicate with others.

对应段落：J段

审题：题目问的是提到在俄亥俄州进行的研究。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的"research conducted in Ohio"定位，在文章中找到提到俄亥俄州的研究内容。

解题思路：J段提到了Peter Miraldi来自俄亥俄州的Kent State University，他正在进行一项关于基因是否影响个体与他人沟通动机的研究，因此J段包含了在俄亥俄州进行的研究信息。
15

15 Medical contribution to the researches for twins
✅ 正确答案：D

原文答案句：And twins can be used, within ethical limits, for medical experiments. A study that administered vitamin C to one twin and a placebo to the other found that it had no effect on the common cold.  

对应段落：D段  

审题：题目问的是关于双胞胎研究的医学贡献。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"Medical contribution"可以定位到文章中关于医学实验和双胞胎研究的内容。  

解题思路：在D段中，明确提到了双胞胎可以在伦理范围内用于医学实验，并且举了一个具体的例子：给一个双胞胎服用维生素C，给另一个服用安慰剂，发现对普通感冒没有效果。这表明双胞胎研究在医学领域有实际的应用和贡献。
16

16 Research situation under life-threatening conditions
✅ 正确答案：E

原文答案句：Josef Mengele, a Nazi doctor working at the Auschwitz extermination camp during the Second World War, was fascinated by twins. He sought them out among arrivals at the camp and preserved them from the gas-chambers for a series of brutal experiments.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问的是在生命威胁条件下进行的研究情况。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"life threatening conditions"（生命威胁条件）可以定位到E段，该段描述了约瑟夫·门格勒在奥斯威辛灭绝营进行的研究，这是一个极端危险的环境。

解题思路：E段提到约瑟夫·门格勒在奥斯威辛灭绝营对双胞胎进行了残酷的实验，这个环境是极其危险的，符合"life threatening conditions"的描述。因此，E段包含了题目所问的信息。
17

17 Data of similarities of identical twins
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：Early work, for example, showed that the statistical correlation of skin-mole counts between identical twins was 0.4, while non-identical twins had a correlation of only 0.2. (A score of 1.0 implies perfect correlation, while a score of zero implies no correlation.)

对应段落：B段

审题：题目问的是关于同卵双胞胎的相似性的数据。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词"Data of similarities"和"identical twins"来定位答案。在B段中，原文提到了"statistical correlation"（统计相关性）和具体的百分比数据"0.4"和"0.2"，这与题干中的"Data of similarities"相对应。

解题思路：原文B段明确提到了同卵双胞胎和非同卵双胞胎在皮肤痣数量上的统计相关性分别为0.4和0.2，并解释了相关性分数的含义。这里的"statistical correlation"可以理解为相似性的数据，而具体的数据"0.4"和"0.2"正是题目所询问的"Data of similarities"的具体表现形式。因此，B段包含了题目所要求的关于同卵双胞胎相似性的数据。
18

18 Reasons that make one study unconvincing
✅ 正确答案：E

原文答案句：After the war, Cyril Burt, a British psychologist who worked on the heredity of intelligence, tainted twin research with results that appear, in retrospect, to have been rather too good. Some of his data on identical twins who had been reared apart were probably faked.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问的是“使一项研究不可信的原因”。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的“unconvincing”一词可以定位到E段，该段提到了Cyril Burt的研究结果存在问题，数据可能是伪造的。

解题思路：原文中明确指出Cyril Burt的研究结果“appear, in retrospect, to have been rather too good”，并且“Some of his data... were probably faked”，这直接说明了这项研究因为数据造假而不具备说服力，因此E段包含了题目所问的信息。

Questions 19-20 


Complete the summary below. 


Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer. Write your answers in boxes 19-20 on your answer sheet.


19

The first one that conducted research on twins is called 19 .

正确答案： 19 Francis Galton
Question 19

原文答案句：Francis Galton, an early geneticist, realized that they came in two varieties: identical twins born from one egg and non-identical twins that had come from two.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是第一个进行双胞胎研究的科学家是谁。

定位关键信息：通过题干中的"the first one that conducted research on twins"可以定位到A段，其中提到了"Francis Galton, an early geneticist"。

解题思路：根据原文句子可知，Francis Galton是最早进行双胞胎研究的科学家，因此答案为Francis Galton。


20

He separated twins into two categories: non-identical and identical twins. 

The twin research was used in medical application in as early as the year of 20 .

正确答案： 20 1924
Question 20

原文答案句：That insight turned out to be key, although it was not until 1924 that it was used to formulate what is known as the twin rule of pathology, and twin studies really got going.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“双胞胎研究在医学应用上最早是在哪一年开始的”。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词“medical application”和“year”来定位原文中关于双胞胎研究在医学上应用的具体年份。

解题思路：原文中提到“it was not until 1924 that it was used to formulate what is known as the twin rule of pathology”，意思是直到1924年，双胞胎研究才被用来制定病理学上的双胞胎规则，这表明双胞胎研究在医学上的应用最早是在1924年。因此，答案为1924。


Questions 21-23

 

Choose THREE correct answers


Options (Word Bank):
A. Sense
B. Cancer
C. Be allergic to Vitamin D
D. Mole heredity
E. Sound
F. Baldness of men
21

Please choose THREE research fields that had been carried out in Ohio, Maryland and Twinsburgh?

正确答案： 1 A. Sense  |  2 E. Sound  |  3 F. Baldness of men

原文答案句：  

- A. Sense: "Paul Breslin, who works at the Monell Institute in Philadelphia, watched over several tables where twins sat sipping clear liquids from cups and making notes. It was the team’s third year at Twinsburg. Dr Breslin and his colleagues want to find out how genes influence human perception, particularly the senses of smell and taste..."  

- E. Sound: "Dennis Drayna, from the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders, in Maryland, was studying hearing."  

- F. Baldness of men: "next to Mr. Miraldi was a team of dermatologists from Case Western Reserve University in Cleveland. They are looking at the development of skin diseases and male-pattern baldness."  

对应段落：  

- A. Sense: G段  

- E. Sound: I段  

- F. Baldness of men: J段  

审题：题目要求选择三个在Ohio、Maryland和Twinsburg进行的科研领域。  

定位关键信息：  

1. 通过地点Ohio、Maryland和Twinsburg定位相关段落。Ohio对应J段（Case Western Reserve University in Cleveland），Maryland对应I段（National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders），Twinsburg对应G段（Twinsburg festival）。  

2. 分别提取各段中的科研领域：G段研究“senses of smell and taste”（对应A. Sense），I段研究“hearing”（对应E. Sound），J段研究“male-pattern baldness”（对应F. Baldness of men）。  

解题思路：  

1. 根据地点Ohio、Maryland和Twinsburg，分别定位到J段、I段和G段。  

2. J段明确提到研究“male-pattern baldness”（男性秃发），对应选项F。  

3. I段提到研究“hearing”（听觉），对应选项E。  

4. G段提到研究“senses of smell and taste”（嗅觉和味觉），对应选项A。  

5. 排除其他选项：B（Cancer）未在指定地点提及，C（Be allergic to Vitamin D）未提及，D（Mole heredity）出现在B段但未在指定地点进行。  

6. 最终确定答案为A、E、F。


Questions 24-26

 

Choose THREE correct answers


Options (Word Bank):
A. Non-identical twins come from different eggs.
B. Genetic relation between identical twins is closer than non-identical ones.
C. Vitamin C has evident effect on a cold.
D. Genetic influence to smoking is superior to environment’s.
E. If a pregnant woman eats too much sweet would lead to skin disease.
F. Hair loss has been found to be connected with skin problem.
24

Please choose THREE results that had been verified in this passage.

正确答案： 1 A. Non-identical twins come from different eggs.  |  2 B. Genetic relation between identical twins is closer than non-identical ones.  |  3 D. Genetic influence to smoking is superior to environment’s.

原文答案句：

- A. "THE scientific study of twins goes back to the late 19th century, when Francis Galton, an early geneticist, realized that they came in two varieties: identical twins born from one egg and non-identical twins that had come from two. "

-B. "The twin rule of pathology states that any heritable disease will be more concordant (that is, more likely to be jointly present or absent) in identical twins than in non-identical twins"

-D. "Twin research has shown that whether or not someone takes up smoking is determined mainly by environmental factors, but once he does so, how much he smokes is largely down to his genes. "

对应段落： 

- A. : A段  

- B. : B段

- D. : C段

审题：题目要求选择三个在文章中已被验证的结果。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"results that had been verified"定位到文章中明确提到的研究结果或结论。  

解题思路：选项A直接对应A段中明确说明的同卵双胞胎来自一个卵子，异卵双胞胎来自两个卵子的科学事实，这是已被验证的结论。选项B对应B段中提到的"any heritable disease will be more concordant in identical twins than in non-identical twins"，即同卵双胞胎的遗传相关性比异卵双胞胎更接近，这也是已被验证的研究结果。选项D对应C段中提到的"but once he does so, how much he smokes is largely down to his genes"，明确说明，虽然是否吸烟主要由环境决定，但吸烟量的多少很大程度上取决于基因。这一结论已被验证。其他选项要么未被提及，要么与原文表述不符。
